fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Bicategories are virtual bicategories with all composites

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A bicategory is the same thing as a virtual bicategory that has all composites, as observed by Derek Elkins.
A directed graph G is defined to be a collection of 0-cells A,B,C, \ldots \in G together with a collection of edges { A } \xrightarrow {{ e }}{ B } \in G.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Free category on a directed graph

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let G be a directed graph; a path in G is defined to be a sequence of compatible edges { \mathopen {} \left [ A_0 \xrightarrow {f_0}A_1 \xrightarrow {f_1} \ldots \xrightarrow {f_{n-1}}A_n \right ] \mathclose {}}. Evidently, the free category G ^{ \star } on G has morphisms given by paths.
A virtual bicategory \mathcal {K} is a directed graph equipped with the following additional data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each path { A } \xrightarrow {{ \Psi }}{ B } \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } and each edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } \in \mathcal {K}, a collection of 2-cells { \Psi } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ f } \in \mathcal {K} { \mathopen {} \left ( A,B \right ) \mathclose {}};

for any edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B }, an identity 2-cell { { \mathopen {} \left [ f \right ] \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ 1 _{ f } }}{ f } \in \mathcal {K} { \mathopen {} \left ( A,B \right ) \mathclose {}};

for any sequence of compatible paths A_0 \xrightarrow { \Psi _0} \ldots \xrightarrow { \Psi _{n-1}}A_n \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } and 2-cells { \Psi _i } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha _i }}{ f_i } \in \mathcal {K} { \mathopen {} \left ( A_i,A_{i+1} \right ) \mathclose {}} and { { \mathopen {} \left [ f_i \mid i<n \right ] \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ \beta }}{ g } \in \mathcal {K} { \mathopen {} \left ( A_0,A_n \right ) \mathclose {}}, a composite 2-cell { \Psi _0; \ldots ; \Psi _n } \xrightarrow {{ { \mathopen {} \left [ \alpha _i \mid i<n \right ] \mathclose {}} ; \beta }}{ g };
such that the composition of 2-cells is both associative and unital with respect to identity 2-cells.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {K} be a virtual bicategory; given a path { A } \xrightarrow {{ \Psi }}{ B } \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } and an edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B }, a 2-cell { \Psi } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ f } is called cocartesian if any 2-cell { \Phi ; \Psi ; \Xi } \xrightarrow {{ \beta }}{ s } factors uniquely through \alpha in the sense that there exists a unique 2-cell { \Phi ; { \mathopen {} \left [ f \right ] \mathclose {}} ; \Xi } \xrightarrow {{ \beta \setminus \alpha }}{ s } such that \beta = { \mathopen {} \left [ \overrightarrow { 1 _{ \Phi _i } \ldots }, \alpha , \overrightarrow { 1 _{ \Xi _i } \ldots } \right ] \mathclose {}} ; { \mathopen {} \left ( \beta \setminus \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Left- and right-cocartesian 2-cells in a virtual bicategory

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {K} be a virtual bicategory; given a path { A } \xrightarrow {{ \Psi }}{ B } \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } and an edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B }, a 2-cell { \Psi } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ f } is called left-cocartesian if for 2-cell { \Psi ; \Xi } \xrightarrow {{ \beta }}{ s } factors uniquely through \alpha in the sense that there exists a unique 2-cell { { \mathopen {} \left [ f \right ] \mathclose {}} ; \Psi } \xrightarrow {{ \beta \setminus \alpha }}{ s } such that \beta = { \mathopen {} \left [ \alpha , \overrightarrow { 1 _{ \Xi _i } \ldots } \right ] \mathclose {}} ; { \mathopen {} \left ( \beta \setminus \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}}.

Dually, given a path { B } \xrightarrow {{ \Psi }}{ C } \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } and an edge { B } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ C }, a 2-cell { \Psi } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ f } is called right-cocartesian if for 2-cell { \Phi ; \Psi } \xrightarrow {{ \beta }}{ s } factors uniquely through \alpha in the sense that there exists a unique 2-cell { \Phi ; { \mathopen {} \left [ f \right ] \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ \beta \setminus \alpha }}{ s } such that \beta = { \mathopen {} \left [ \overrightarrow { 1 _{ \Phi _i } \ldots }, \alpha \right ] \mathclose {}} ; { \mathopen {} \left ( \beta \setminus \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}}.
These definitions are due to Arkor and McDermott.
Let \mathcal {K} be a virtual bicategory, and let { A } \xrightarrow {{ \Psi }}{ B } \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } be a path in \mathcal {K}. Then we define:

fr:ol.fr:li: a composite of \Psi is a cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi;
a left half-composite of \Psi is a left-cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi;
a right half-composite of \Psi is a right-cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Bicategories are virtual bicategories with all composites

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A bicategory is the same thing as a virtual bicategory that has all composites, as observed by Derek Elkins.

I still don't know if the following are the correct definitions. One would have to check that the left-composite of a pair of maps is isomorphic to the right-composite if both exist, which sounds plausible.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Thunkable and linear maps in a virtual bicategory

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {K} be a virtual bicategory; we isolate two classes of edges:

fr:ol.fr:li: an edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } in \mathcal {K} is called thunkable if and only if for any edge { B } \xrightarrow {{ g }}{ C } there exists a left half-composite { { \mathopen {} \left [ f;g \right ] \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ \lambda }}{ h };

dually, an edge { B } \xrightarrow {{ g }}{ C } in \mathcal {K} is called linear if and only if for any edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } there exists a right half-composite { { \mathopen {} \left [ f;g \right ] \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ \rho }}{ h }.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {K} be a virtual bicategory; we isolate two classes of objects in \mathcal {K}, by analogy with the case for deductive systems:

fr:ol.fr:li: an object B \in \mathcal {K} is called positive when every edge { B } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ C } is linear;
an object B \in \mathcal {K} is called negative when every edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is thunkable.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Then we say that an object is polarized when it is either positive or negative in the above sense.
Let \mathcal {K} be a virtual bicategory, and let { A } \xrightarrow {{ \Psi }}{ B } \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } be a path in \mathcal {K}. Then we define:

fr:ol.fr:li: a composite of \Psi is a cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi;
a left half-composite of \Psi is a left-cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi;
a right half-composite of \Psi is a right-cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A virtual bicategory \mathcal {K} is a directed graph equipped with the following additional data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each path { A } \xrightarrow {{ \Psi }}{ B } \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } and each edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } \in \mathcal {K}, a collection of 2-cells { \Psi } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ f } \in \mathcal {K} { \mathopen {} \left ( A,B \right ) \mathclose {}};

for any edge { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B }, an identity 2-cell { { \mathopen {} \left [ f \right ] \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ 1 _{ f } }}{ f } \in \mathcal {K} { \mathopen {} \left ( A,B \right ) \mathclose {}};

for any sequence of compatible paths A_0 \xrightarrow { \Psi _0} \ldots \xrightarrow { \Psi _{n-1}}A_n \in \mathcal {K} ^{ \star } and 2-cells { \Psi _i } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha _i }}{ f_i } \in \mathcal {K} { \mathopen {} \left ( A_i,A_{i+1} \right ) \mathclose {}} and { { \mathopen {} \left [ f_i \mid i<n \right ] \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ \beta }}{ g } \in \mathcal {K} { \mathopen {} \left ( A_0,A_n \right ) \mathclose {}}, a composite 2-cell { \Psi _0; \ldots ; \Psi _n } \xrightarrow {{ { \mathopen {} \left [ \alpha _i \mid i<n \right ] \mathclose {}} ; \beta }}{ g };
such that the composition of 2-cells is both associative and unital with respect to identity 2-cells.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A bicategory B is given by the following structure and axioms:

fr:ol.fr:li: a collection of objects x,y,z called 0-cells;
for each pair of 0-cells x,y a category \mathbf {hom} _{ B } { \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {}} of morphisms or 1-cells x \xrightarrow {f}y;
for each object x, a morphism x \xrightarrow { 1 _{ x } }x called the identity morphism;

for all objects x,y,z, a functor \mathbf {hom} _{ B } { \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {}} \times \mathbf {hom} _{ B } { \mathopen {} \left ( y , z \right ) \mathclose {}} \xrightarrow { { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} \right ) \mathclose {}} } \mathbf {hom} _{ B } { \mathopen {} \left ( x , z \right ) \mathclose {}} called horizontal composition;

for all 1-morphisms e \xrightarrow {f}x \xrightarrow {g}y \xrightarrow {h}z, an isomorphism { \mathopen {} \left ( f \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} g \right ) \mathclose {}} \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} h \xrightarrow { \alpha _{ f,g,h } } f \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} { \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} h \right ) \mathclose {}} natural in f,g,h called the associator;
for all 1-morphisms x \xrightarrow {f}y, an isomorphism 1 _{ x } \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} f \xrightarrow { \lambda _{ f } } f natural in f called the left unitor;
for all 1-morphisms x \xrightarrow {f}y, an isomorphism f \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} 1 _{ y } \xrightarrow { \rho _{ f } } f natural in f called the right unitor;
